weekly_brief_narrative:
The lead development this week is the EU Commission's signing of first ReArm Europe EDIP contracts worth €500M for joint ammunition production, marking a shift from rhetoric to tangible defence industrial capacity under P1 calibration rules. This dominates as it directly addresses Ukraine sustainment gaps and US supply dependency, with contracts to Rheinmetall-Nexter consortia targeting 1M shells by 2028.

Defence integration advances incrementally via these procurements, though full autonomy remains at Lagrange 3 pending scaled production. Energy dependency holds stable at 3, with raw materials MoUs diversifying lithium sources from China but offset by persistent US LNG volumes per P2 mixed baseline. Tech sovereignty deteriorates structurally to 2, as no enforcement actions counter US Big Tech DSA pressures. Trade faces acute deterioration from US tariff hikes to 30% on steel, activating 2026 US coercion vector in multiple domains. Diplomatic dynamics remain stable at 3, buoyed by routine NATO-EU hybrid coordination.

Hybrid threats intensified with two notable incidents: Russian FIMI sustaining pre-election disruption in Germany (High confidence, pattern-linked) and Belarusian sabotage probes on Polish rail (Assessed). These fit episodic grey-zone patterns without structural upgrade yet, though coverage meets minimum via Tier1 sources.

US-EU relations entered heightened friction via USTR tariff actions, with no counterbalancing cooperation beyond NATO talks; trajectory weakens per calibration's multi-vector squeeze rule. NATO-EU staff exchanges provided marginal stability.

Monitor in coming days: USTR auto tariff decision (April 20 threshold), German regional vote FIMI spikes, EDIP disbursement approvals, and Polish-Belarus border incident follow-up. Thresholds include tariff quota breaches triggering retaliation and raw materials pact ratifications.
